3.happening: Start with an empty stack — it will hold operands waiting to be combined.
3.why: A stack hands back the two most-recent operands in O(1), exactly what each operator needs.
3.invariant: The stack holds every value not yet consumed, in the order it arrived.

4.happening: List the four operator symbols so we can tell operators from numbers.
4.why: A membership test decides how each token is handled.
4.invariant: A token is an operator exactly when it is in this set.

5.why: Reverse Polish Notation is evaluated by scanning tokens left to right.
5.invariant: Every token before this one is already reflected on the stack.

6.why: Numbers get pushed; operators consume the top two values.
6.invariant: Each token is either an operator or a number.

7.happening: Pop the top value into b — the right-hand operand.
7.why: The most recently pushed value is the operator's right operand.
7.invariant: After this pop, b holds the right operand and the stack is one shorter.

8.happening: Pop the next value into a — the left-hand operand.
8.why: The value beneath is the left operand; order matters for subtraction and division.
8.invariant: a is the left operand, b the right.

9.why: Each operator applies a different arithmetic rule.
9.invariant: Exactly one of the four operator branches will run.

10.why: Addition combines the two popped values.

11.why: Subtraction is order-sensitive, so operand order matters.
11.invariant: Reached only when the token is not '+'.

12.why: The deeper operand a is the minuend, b the subtrahend.

13.why: Only division remains if this test also fails.
13.invariant: Reached only when the token is neither '+' nor '-'.

16.why: The problem requires integer division that rounds toward zero.
16.invariant: value will hold the truncated quotient.

17.happening: Push the result {value} back onto the stack.
17.why: The combined value becomes an operand for later operators.
17.invariant: The stack shrank by two and grew by one — a net of one fewer value.

19.happening: '{token}' is a number, so convert it to an integer.
19.why: Tokens arrive as strings and must become numbers before arithmetic.
19.invariant: value will hold the integer form of the token.

20.why: Operands wait on the stack until an operator consumes them.

21.happening: All tokens processed — one value remains ({stack[0]}), the answer.
21.why: A valid RPN expression collapses to exactly one value.
21.invariant: The stack holds exactly one element.

22.happening: Return the value of the expression: {answer}.
22.why: That single remaining value is the result.
22.invariant: The function exits with the expression's value.

init.happening: Setting up the empty operand stack.
init.why: The stack will hold values until operators combine them.

loop.why: RPN is evaluated left to right.
loop.invariant: Processed tokens are already reflected on the stack.

check.happening: Deciding whether the token is an operator and, if so, which one.
check.why: Operators and numbers are handled differently.
check.invariant: Each token routes to exactly one action.

update.happening: Updating the stack — pushing a number, or popping two operands and pushing their result.
update.why: The stack always holds the operands still waiting to be used.
update.invariant: Each operator reduces the stack by one net value.

return.why: A valid expression reduces to one value.
return.invariant: The answer is the last value on the stack.
